João, I have the dashboard open, so don't walk me through the whole project. Give me where we stand — schedule and cost, one line each.

The bottom line: schedule is green, cost is amber. One package — the axle counters — came in over budget, a cost variance of four percent, above your two-percent threshold. Everything else is on plan.
Good — you named it before I had to. Why is it over?

Two units failed the incoming inspection, so we expedited replacements. That pushed the burn rate up in April, and at that run rate the package lands about four percent over. The earned value is at sixty-two against a plan of sixty-six, so we're a little behind on completed work too.
I don't want history, I want a date. When is this variance gone?

By the end of Q3 we will have completed the site acceptance tests, and by the time the board meets again we will have closed the variance. This time next month the crew will be running the SATs on nights, so the recovery is already in motion — not a plan on a slide.
And the smaller packages — do any of them help me here?

Two came in under budget and let us nearly break even on the minor scope — but honestly, that doesn't move the needle on the total. The number that matters is the axle-counter variance, and that's the one I've committed a date to.
Let me lead with the bottom line before the detail.
The axle-counter package came in over budget — the variance is above threshold, and it's mine to explain.
By the end of Q3 we will have completed the site acceptance tests.
This time next month the crew will be running the SATs on nights.
That's a ballpark figure, not the forecast — I'll have the firm number by Friday.

João, before you present the numbers to the programme board tomorrow, let me tell you how a board actually listens, because engineers get this exactly backwards. You want to walk them through the whole story, the context, the cause, and arrive at the number at the end, like a proof. Do not. A board reads the number first and the story second. So lead with the bottom line. In the first sentence, tell them where the project stands against plan and against budget, one KPI, one figure. Then, and only then, the why. Second thing: own the deviation before they find it. You have a cost variance above threshold, the axle-counter package came in over budget. Say so, plainly, in your own voice, before Ingrid points at it on the dashboard. The number that the presenter names first is a number under control; the number the board discovers is a number out of control, even when it is the same number. Third, and this is where your grammar earns its keep, a forecast is not a hope, it is a commitment, so say it like one. Do not say we hope to finish the tests by the third quarter. Say by the end of Q3 we will have completed the site acceptance tests, and by the time the board meets again we will have closed the variance. That is the future perfect, and it lands as a promise with a date on it. And when they ask what the team is doing right now to recover, use the future continuous: this time next month the commissioning crew will be running the SATs on nights. It tells them the recovery is already in motion, not a plan on a slide. Lead with the bottom line, own the overrun in your own words, and forecast in dates you will actually hit. The numbers do not defend themselves. You do.

Three things about reading project numbers, from someone who has built a lot of these dashboards. First, know the difference between the burn rate and the run rate, because people mix them and it costs them credibility. The burn rate is how fast you are spending right now, this month's drawdown from the budget. The run rate is that pace projected forward, what the whole year looks like if this month simply continues. A board does not want this month's spend; it wants the run rate, because the run rate tells them where you land. Second, earned value is the one number that keeps you honest. It is not how much you have spent and it is not how much time has passed; it is the budgeted value of the work you have actually finished. You can be halfway through the calendar and halfway through the money and still be badly behind, because only a third of the work is truly done. The cost variance is the gap between what that finished work was supposed to cost and what it actually cost, and once it crosses the threshold, it goes red on the board and someone has to explain it. Third, treat a ballpark figure and a forecast as two completely different animals. A ballpark is a rough number you give early, when nobody should hold you to it, and you must say out loud that it is a ballpark. A forecast is a commitment, updated with real data, and you state it in the future perfect: by quarter end we will have closed the gap. If you present a ballpark as if it were a forecast, you will spend the next three meetings explaining why the real number moved. Lead with the bottom line, know whether a number moves the needle or not, and never promise in the future perfect anything you have not actually planned to deliver.
